why: why use event delegation instead of multiple listeners
how: how to handle clicks on dynamic elements
output: single listener handles clicks on all children

1_beginner_explanation.summary: **Event Delegation** is a clever performance trick that takes advantage of Event Bubbling. Instead of attaching 1,000 click listeners to 1,000 buttons in a list, you attach just ONE click listener to the parent `<ul>`. Because clicks on the buttons 'bubble up' to the `<ul>`, the parent can catch the event and use `e.target` to figure out which specific button was clicked.
1_beginner_explanation.analogy: Imagine a classroom with 30 students. If you want to know when a student raises their hand, you don't hire 30 assistants to watch one student each. You just stand at the front of the room (the Parent element) and watch the whole room (Delegation). When someone raises a hand (the Event), you look at exactly who did it (`e.target`).

2_internal_mechanism.execution_flow: The engine only has to instantiate and track a single C++ listener object on the parent element. When the event bubbles up, the callback executes and evaluates conditional logic (`if (e.target.matches(...))`) to determine routing.
2_internal_mechanism.memory: Delegation drastically reduces memory consumption because you avoid creating thousands of listener objects and closures. It also prevents memory leaks when child elements are destroyed and recreated.

3_real_world_example.use_case: Handling clicks on elements that are generated dynamically (like a shopping cart or to-do list).

4_common_confusion.trap: Clicking an icon *inside* the button. If your button has an `<i class="icon">` inside it, `e.target` will be the `<i>`, not the `<button>`. Your `e.target.classList.contains('btn')` check will fail.
4_common_confusion.fix: Use `.closest()` to ensure you grab the button even if a child icon was clicked: `const btn = e.target.closest('.btn'); if (btn) { ... }`.

6_edge_case.quirk: Event delegation relies entirely on Bubbling. It does not work for events that don't bubble, such as `focus`, `blur`, `scroll`, or `mouseenter`.
6_edge_case.code:
// You cannot delegate a 'mouseenter' event using normal bubbling.
// You must use 'mouseover' instead, which does bubble.
